title: He Aromātai Āhuarangi mō te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Climate Impact Assessment of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant waterway flowing through Ngāti Kahungunu rohe. The stream holds cultural importance as a traditional food gathering site and features in local pūrākau about the taniwha Hinekorako, guardian of the waters. Students will connect scientific monitoring with mātauranga Māori perspectives on water guardianship (kaitiakitanga).
learningObjectives: Collect and analyze water quality data (pH, nitrate levels, turbidity) from three sites along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using scientific equipment
Compare scientific water quality data with mātauranga Māori indicators of stream health (presence of native species, water clarity, riparian vegetation)
Develop a comprehensive assessment report that integrates scientific findings with cultural perspectives to evaluate the stream's current health
Propose evidence-based restoration actions that combine modern scientific approaches with traditional Māori environmental practices

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa (Connecting with the Stream)
activities.description: Visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua who will share pūrākau (traditional stories) about Hinekorako and demonstrate traditional methods of assessing water health. Students make initial observations and record cultural indicators of stream wellbeing.

activities.title: Pūtaiao In Situ (Field Science Investigation)
activities.description: Working in small groups, students collect water samples from upstream, mid-stream, and downstream sites. They measure pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, temperature, and dissolved oxygen using water testing kits. Students also document macroinvertebrate diversity as bioindicators of water health.

activities.title: Whakamātau Taiwhanga (Laboratory Analysis)
activities.description: Back in the science lab, students analyze their water samples more precisely, create data tables and graphs of their findings, and compare results across sampling sites. They research how different pollutant levels affect aquatic ecosystems.

activities.title: Hui Arotake (Assessment Meeting)
activities.description: Students participate in a simulated community hui where they present their scientific findings alongside cultural observations. They discuss what the data means for the health of the awa and begin to develop recommendations for action.

activities.title: Mahere Whakaora (Restoration Planning)
activities.description: Based on their assessment, student groups develop specific restoration plans that combine scientific approaches (riparian planting to reduce nitrate runoff) with cultural practices (reintroducing traditional food species). They create visual presentations of their proposed solutions.

resources: Hawke's Bay Regional Council Water Quality Monitoring Kit (pH meters, nitrate test strips, turbidity tubes)
Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua Hemi Te Whare as cultural guide and knowledge holder
NIWA's Freshwater Fish Database and Stream Health Monitoring Guide
Local documentary 'Te Mana o te Wai' featuring Mangakōtukutuku stream restoration projects

tasks: Scientific field report with data tables and graphs of water quality measurements
Comparative analysis paragraph linking scientific data to cultural indicators of stream health
Multimedia presentation proposing specific restoration actions for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Investigating in science, Participating and contributing
Living World: Ecology - Investigate the impact of natural events and human actions on a New Zealand ecosystem
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems - Investigate the water cycle and its dynamic relationship with other Earth systems
Science Capabilities: Gather and interpret data, Use evidence, Critique evidence
